refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication packet loss at or below the customer-defined threshold (acceptance criterion: ≤1% under agreed network conditions) to ensure safe and effective remote equipment operation.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-009 addresses a critical quality-of-service requirement for teleoperation. Packet loss directly impacts operator situational awareness, control responsiveness, and safety. Refining the FR to explicitly reference the acceptance criterion (≤1%) and network conditions scope clarifies the verification boundary and prevents ambiguity in acceptance testing.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct packet loss measurement under representative network conditions (latency, bandwidth, interference profiles agreed with customer). Measure end-to-end packet loss across Telemetry (sensor→operator) and Tasking (operator→vehicle) paths. Acceptance: measured packet loss ≤ customer-defined limit across 95th percentile of test scenarios.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation system shall reliably transmit sensor telemetry from vehicle to operator with minimal loss.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation system shall reliably transmit operator commands from operator to vehicle with minimal loss.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation system shall detect and report communication quality degradation (packet loss threshold breach) to safety services.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement redundant communication paths (dual network links with automatic failover) to reduce single-point-of-failure packet loss impact.
DP-B: Deploy adaptive quality-of-service (QoS) prioritization and packet scheduling to minimize loss of critical telemetry and tasking messages.
DP-C: Integrate forward error correction (FEC) and packet retransmission protocols at the application layer to recover lost packets.
DP-D: Combine network-level redundancy (DP-A), QoS prioritization (DP-B), and application-layer recovery (DP-C) in a layered architecture.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Implement a layered packet loss mitigation architecture combining (1) redundant communication paths with automatic failover, (2) QoS-based prioritization of telemetry and tasking traffic, and (3) application-layer forward error correction and selective retransmission for critical messages. This multi-layer approach addresses packet loss at network, transport, and application levels, providing defense-in-depth and meeting high safety criticality requirements.

potential_sources: Coupling between redundancy failover logic (DP-A) and QoS scheduler (DP-B): failover events may temporarily disrupt QoS state; mitigation via state synchronization protocol.
Coupling between application-layer FEC (DP-C) and network-layer redundancy (DP-A): dual paths may carry redundant FEC packets; mitigation via intelligent packet scheduling to avoid duplication.
Coupling with Safety Services: packet loss detection (FR-NGHE-009.3) must trigger SafetyAlert interface; design must ensure alert latency meets safety response time requirements.
coupling_check.notes: Layered architecture is well-established in telecommunications and avionics. Couplings are manageable via clear interface contracts and state management protocols. High safety criticality justifies the complexity of DP-D over simpler alternatives.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Customer-defined packet loss threshold and network conditions are formally documented and stable; if threshold changes, DP-D may require recalibration.
ASSUMPTION: Dual network links (e.g., cellular + satellite, or dual cellular carriers) are available at the worksite; if not, DP-A redundancy cannot be realized.

RISK: FEC overhead (DP-C) increases bandwidth consumption; if network bandwidth is severely constrained, FEC may paradoxically increase packet loss due to congestion. Mitigation: adaptive FEC tuning based on real-time network metrics.

RISK: Application-layer retransmission (DP-C) introduces latency; if operator control loop latency becomes excessive, safety may be compromised. Mitigation: strict latency budgeting and selective retransmission (only for non-time-critical telemetry).

RISK: Failover logic (DP-A) may introduce transient packet loss during switchover; if switchover time exceeds operator tolerance, safety impact. Mitigation: sub-100ms failover target and operator notification.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-D to explicitly specify: (1) failover detection and switchover time budget (target: <100ms), (2) FEC algorithm selection and overhead budget (target: <10% bandwidth increase), (3) retransmission policy (e.g., only for SafetyAlert and critical Tasking, not all Telemetry), (4) QoS priority levels (e.g., SafetyAlert > Tasking > Telemetry), and (5) monitoring/alerting thresholds for packet loss detection (e.g., alert at 0.5% to provide margin before 1% acceptance limit).

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the end-to-end communication quality assurance for remote equipment operation, including redundant network path management, QoS scheduling, and application-layer packet recovery. Scope includes: (1) dual network link abstraction and failover logic, (2) packet prioritization and scheduling engine, (3) FEC encoding/decoding and selective retransmission, (4) packet loss monitoring and metrics collection, (5) SafetyAlert generation on quality degradation. Scope excludes: physical network infrastructure (owned by Vehicle or external provider), operator interface (owned by Tele-operator domain), and vehicle control execution (owned by Vehicle module).

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Inbound sensor data from Vehicle module (e.g., position, orientation, load, camera feed). Teleoperation module applies FEC encoding and QoS prioritization before transmission over redundant network links. Expected packet rate: ~100 Hz for critical sensors, ~10 Hz for non-critical. Packet loss target: ≤1% end-to-end.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Outbound operator commands from Tele-operator to Vehicle module (e.g., movement, bucket control, emergency stop). Teleoperation module applies QoS prioritization and retransmission logic. Expected packet rate: ~50 Hz for real-time control, ~1 Hz for mode changes. Packet loss target: ≤1% end-to-end.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Dual redundant network paths (e.g., cellular + satellite, or dual cellular carriers). Teleoperation module manages failover logic, monitors link quality (latency, jitter, packet loss per link), and distributes traffic across active links. Each link may have different characteristics; Teleoperation abstracts this heterogeneity.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Outbound safety alerts to Safety Services module when packet loss exceeds threshold (e.g., >0.5% sustained loss, or >1% instantaneous loss). Alert includes: loss percentage, affected path (Telemetry vs. Tasking), recommended action (operator intervention, failover status, emergency stop). Latency requirement: <500ms from detection to alert delivery.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation is the natural owner of packet loss mitigation logic. All DP-D components (redundancy, QoS, FEC, retransmission, monitoring) are cohesive and belong in this module. Minimal diffusion: only SafetyAlert interface crosses boundary to Safety Services (5% diffusion for alert generation logic).

Vehicle module owns vehicle control and sensor acquisition, not communication quality. Assigning DP-D to Vehicle would scatter communication logic across vehicle firmware, creating tight coupling with vehicle control logic and making packet loss mitigation difficult to test independently. High diffusion risk.

Safety Services owns safety policy enforcement and alerts, not communication infrastructure. Assigning DP-D to Safety Services would conflate communication quality assurance with safety decision-making, violating separation of concerns. Safety Services should consume SafetyAlert from Teleoperation, not own the mitigation logic.

NGHE Solution is the top-level system container. While DP-D is part of the overall solution, assigning it to NGHE Solution (rather than delegating to Teleoperation) would prevent proper architectural layering and make the solution-level module too large and unfocused. Teleoperation is the appropriate subsystem owner.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the optimal owner of DP-D (layered packet loss mitigation) because: (1) it is functionally cohesive—all components (redundancy, QoS, FEC, retransmission, monitoring) serve the single purpose of ensuring communication quality, (2) it has clear external interfaces (Telemetry in, Tasking out, Network Link inout, SafetyAlert out), (3) it can be developed, tested, and deployed independently of vehicle control logic, (4) it aligns with the existing NGHE architecture where Teleoperation is the communication subsystem, and (5) it minimizes diffusion of packet loss logic across multiple modules.
containment_risks: RISK: Teleoperation module may become large and complex if DP-D is fully realized within it. Mitigation: decompose DP-D into sub-modules (e.g., RedundancyManager, QoSScheduler, FECCodec, PacketLossMonitor) within Teleoperation boundary, with clear internal interfaces.

RISK: Teleoperation module depends on Network Link interface, which may be provided by external network provider or Vehicle module. If Network Link interface is unstable or poorly specified, Teleoperation's ability to meet packet loss target is compromised. Mitigation: establish formal SLA for Network Link interface (latency, jitter, per-link packet loss).

RISK: SafetyAlert interface to Safety Services creates a dependency on Safety Services' alert processing latency. If Safety Services is slow to process alerts, operator response may be delayed. Mitigation: specify SafetyAlert latency budget (<500ms) and ensure Safety Services meets it.

RISK: Dual network links may not be available in all worksites (e.g., remote locations with only one cellular carrier). If DP-A (redundancy) cannot be realized, packet loss target may not be achievable. Mitigation: define fallback DP for single-link scenarios (e.g., enhanced FEC, more aggressive retransmission) and document worksite prerequisites.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Refine IM assignment to specify: (1) Teleoperation module internal architecture (sub-modules: RedundancyManager, QoSScheduler, FECCodec, PacketLossMonitor), (2) Network Link interface SLA (per-link packet loss, latency, jitter targets), (3) SafetyAlert latency budget and Safety Services integration protocol, (4) worksite prerequisites for dual network links, and (5) fallback strategies for single-link scenarios. Additionally, establish a configuration management process for customer-defined packet loss thresholds to ensure DP-D can be tuned per customer without code changes.

fr_dp_gate.reason: FR-NGHE-009 (packet loss ≤1% under agreed network conditions) is directly addressed by DP-D (layered mitigation via redundancy, QoS, FEC, retransmission). DP-D is a complete and sufficient solution to the FR. No independence violations detected: DP-D does not over-constrain the design (e.g., it does not mandate specific network technologies, only requires dual paths and QoS capability). Coupling between DP-A, DP-B, DP-C is manageable via clear interface contracts. FR is verifiable: packet loss can be measured end-to-end under agreed network conditions.

diffusion_risks: SafetyAlert generation logic (part of DP-D monitoring) crosses boundary to Safety Services. Mitigation: SafetyAlert interface is well-defined; Teleoperation owns detection and alert generation, Safety Services owns alert processing and response.
Network Link abstraction (part of DP-A redundancy) depends on external network provider or Vehicle module. Mitigation: Network Link interface SLA must be formally specified and monitored.
FEC and retransmission logic (DP-C) may interact with operator control loop latency. Mitigation: strict latency budgeting and selective retransmission policy (only for non-time-critical messages).
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module contains 95% of DP-D logic (redundancy, QoS, FEC, retransmission, monitoring). Only 5% diffusion: SafetyAlert interface to Safety Services. This is acceptable because SafetyAlert is a clean, well-defined interface. No logic duplication or scattering across modules. Teleoperation can be developed and tested independently.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: FR-NGHE-009 is verifiable via end-to-end packet loss measurement under agreed network conditions. Teleoperation module provides packet loss metrics (via PacketLossMonitor sub-module) that can be collected and analyzed. Acceptance test: measure packet loss across Telemetry and Tasking paths under representative network scenarios (latency, bandwidth, interference profiles). Acceptance criterion: measured packet loss ≤ customer-defined limit (default 1%) across 95th percentile of test scenarios.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: FR-NGHE-009 is clearly allocated to Teleoperation module. Sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-009.1, 009.2, 009.3) are allocated to specific DP-D components: FR-009.1 → Telemetry path (FEC, retransmission, QoS), FR-009.2 → Tasking path (QoS, retransmission, redundancy), FR-009.3 → PacketLossMonitor and SafetyAlert. Traceability is clear and complete.

recommended_revisions: Establish formal SLA for Network Link interface: per-link packet loss target, latency budget, jitter tolerance, failover detection time. This SLA is critical to Teleoperation's ability to meet FR-NGHE-009.

Define packet loss detection and alert thresholds: e.g., alert at 0.5% sustained loss (to provide margin before 1% acceptance limit), emergency stop at >2% loss. Thresholds should be configurable per customer.
Specify failover logic and switchover time budget: target <100ms switchover time, with operator notification. Ensure failover does not introduce unacceptable latency spike.

Document worksite prerequisites: dual network links required for DP-A redundancy. If only single link available, define fallback DP (enhanced FEC, more aggressive retransmission) and document packet loss target achievability under single-link scenario.
Establish configuration management for customer-defined packet loss thresholds: ensure DP-D can be tuned per customer (e.g., 1% for standard, 0.5% for high-safety applications) without code changes.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-009 is refined, DP-D is selected and passes coupling checks, and Teleoperation module is assigned with high containment score (95%) and minimal diffusion (5%). All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass. However, DP-D is complex and comprises multiple sub-components (redundancy, QoS, FEC, retransmission, monitoring) that warrant further functional decomposition. Additionally, sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-009.1, 009.2, 009.3) have been identified but not yet decomposed. Proceeding to the next level will clarify the internal architecture of Teleoperation module and ensure each sub-component is properly specified and allocated.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation system shall reliably transmit sensor telemetry from vehicle to operator with minimal loss (target: ≤1% packet loss on Telemetry path).
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation system shall reliably transmit operator commands from operator to vehicle with minimal loss (target: ≤1% packet loss on Tasking path).
FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation system shall detect and report communication quality degradation (packet loss threshold breach) to safety services with latency <500ms.

assumptions: Customer-defined packet loss threshold (default 1%) and network conditions are formally documented and stable.
Dual network links (e.g., cellular + satellite, or dual cellular carriers) are available at the worksite.
Network Link interface provides per-link packet loss, latency, and jitter metrics for monitoring and failover decisions.
Safety Services module can process SafetyAlert messages with latency <500ms.
Operator control loop can tolerate application-layer retransmission latency (estimated <100ms for non-critical telemetry).
FEC overhead is acceptable (estimated <10% bandwidth increase) and does not cause congestion-induced packet loss.
open_risks: Dual network links may not be available in all worksites; fallback DP for single-link scenarios needs definition.
Failover switchover time may exceed operator tolerance if not carefully designed; <100ms target is aggressive and requires validation.
FEC and retransmission logic may introduce latency that impacts operator control responsiveness; latency budgeting is critical.
SafetyAlert interface latency depends on Safety Services implementation; if Safety Services is slow, operator response may be delayed.
Packet loss detection thresholds (e.g., 0.5% alert, 2% emergency stop) are not yet defined; customer input required.
evidence_needed: Formal specification of Network Link interface SLA (per-link packet loss, latency, jitter targets).
Formal specification of customer-defined packet loss threshold and network conditions (e.g., latency range, bandwidth, interference profile).
Failover logic design and switchover time validation (target: <100ms).
FEC algorithm selection and overhead analysis (target: <10% bandwidth increase).
Latency budget analysis for operator control loop (including retransmission latency).
SafetyAlert latency budget and Safety Services integration protocol.
Worksite prerequisites and fallback DP for single-link scenarios.
Packet loss detection and alert threshold definition (configurable per customer).
End-to-end packet loss measurement under representative network scenarios (acceptance test plan).
